result.markdown:
--- title: "Preparing (gg)plots for publication: part II" subtitle: "Formatting, multi-panel layouts, and saving plots" pagetitle: "ggplot publication prep II" author: - name: Jelmer Poelstra orcid: 0000-0002-3514-7462 email: poelstra.1@osu.edu affiliation: - name: CFAES Bioinformatics Core, Ohio State University url: https://mcic.osu.edu/bioinformatics date: "2026-03-09" image: featured_plot.png categories: [ggplot2, dataviz, plotting, tidyverse] title-block-banner: false number-depth: 3 execute: eval: true cache: false editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Introduction ### What we'll cover Today, we'll continue on the topic of preparing your plots for publication. Specifically, we'll cover: - **Saving plots to file** -- with _ggplot_'s `ggsave()` _(moved from last weeksince we didn't get there)_ - **ggtext** -- for formatting text in plots with Markdown and HTML - Adding any kind of image to a plot, including: - An image inside a plot - A plot of an image in a multi-panel layout ### Setup #### Loading packages We'll start by loading the _tidyverse_, which includes _ggplot2_, and _palmerpenguins_, which contains the penguins dataset we've been working with: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyverse) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ── ✔ dplyr 1.2.0 ✔ readr 2.2.0 ✔ forcats 1.0.1 ✔ stringr 1.6.0 ✔ ggplot2 4.0.2 ✔ tibble 3.3.1 ✔ lubridate 1.9.5 ✔ tidyr 1.3.2 ✔ purrr 1.2.1 ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ── ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter() ✖ dplyr::lag() masks stats::lag() ℹ Use the conflicted package () to force all conflicts to become errors ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} library(palmerpenguins) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins' The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets': penguins, penguins_raw ``` ::: ::: Like last week, we will load the _patchwork_ package as well, which provides a convenient syntax for multi-panel layouts: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(patchwork) ``` ::: #### Setting the theme Like we learned last week, we can set a plotting theme with `theme_set()`, and customize the theme with `theme_update()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} theme_set(theme_classic(base_size = 13)) theme_update( panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.2) ) ``` ::: #### A base plot We'll also create the same basic plot as last week, so that we have a plot to work with when saving plots: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p <- penguins |> drop_na() |> # Get rid of rows with NAs ggplot(aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) + geom_point(aes(fill = species), shape = 21, size = 2) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) + labs(x = "Flipper length (mm)", y = "Body mass (g)") p ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672} ::: :::
## Saving plots to file So far, we printed plots to the screen in RStudio but did not save them to file. You may have noticed the "Export" button in the Plots pane — it works, but a more flexible and reproducible approach is the `ggsave()` function. ### Basic usage Tell `ggsave()` about the filename and the plot you want to save: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave(filename = "plot.png", plot = p) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Saving 7 x 5 in image ``` ::: ::: - The **file type** is determined by the extension you provide. Above, we used `.png` i.e. a PNG file, but there are other options, as we'll see in a bit. - We did not specify the `width` and `height` of the figure above, so it reports saving it to a default size of 7 × 5 inches. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} #### Saving plots without specifying the plot object _(Click to expand)_ By default, `ggsave()` saves the last plot you produced, and you only need to specify the filename: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave(filename = "plot.png") ``` ::: This can e.g. be handy because you may not have saved your plot to an object. ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} #### Saving the file in a different folder on your computer _(Click to expand)_ In both cases above, the file would be saved to the folder that represents your current "working directory" (directory = folder) in R. But you can save the file to any location on your computer by specifying the path as part of the filename: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Save to a "figures" subfolder in the current working directory: ggsave(filename = "figures/plot.png", plot = p) # Save to a specific folder on your computer: ggsave(filename = "C:/Users/YourName/Documents/figures/plot.png", plot = p) ``` ::: To learn more about file paths and how R deals with them, see [this Code Club session from last fall](/posts/S10E03_reprod_03/). If the folder you're trying to save to does not already exist, a prompt like this will appear to create the folder: ![](img/ggsave_create-folder.png){width="45%" fig-align="center"} :::
### Controlling figure size `ggsave()` accepts `width` and `height` arguments, with a default unit of inches^[ You can change this with the `units` argument, other options are: (`"cm"`, `"mm"`, or `"px"`).]: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave(filename = "wide.png", plot = p, width = 6, height = 4) ``` ::: The figure's size may not seem to matter much, because you could always resize the figure on the computer, right? In fact, what does an inch even mean in the context of a figure displayed on a screen? But it matters quite a bit: for example, for the **aspect ratio** and the **relative text size**. #### Aspect ratio The figure's `width` relative to the `height`, somewhat obviously, determines its aspect ratio -- and this can be useful to customize. Above, our figure is 50% wider than it is high, and that is sensible, because we have a legend on the right-hand side of the plot, and that takes up some horizontal space. But for the sake of practice, let's save the same plot with a different aspect ratio: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave(filename = "tall.png", plot = p, width = 4, height = 6) ``` ::: Here is what the resulting figures should look like: :::: columns ::: {.column width="48%"} ![](wide.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="4%"} ::: ::: {.column width="48%"} ![](tall.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"} ::: :::: #### Relative text size **Text and points do not scale with the figure** — they stay at their absolute sizes (in points/mm). This means: - A plot saved at 3 × 3 will have large-looking text relative to the data - A plot saved at 10 × 8 will have small-looking text This behavior may seem strange but is actually useful: if your text looks too large or too small in the saved file, you can adjust `width` and `height` rather than modifying the plotting code. Let's see this in action, too: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # "Small" figure — text will look relatively large ggsave(filename = "small.png", plot = p, width = 4.5, height = 3) # "Large" figure — text will look relatively small ggsave(filename = "large.png", plot = p, width = 9, height = 6) ``` ::: Here is what the resulting figures should look like: :::: columns ::: {.column width="48%"} ![](small.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="4%"} ::: ::: {.column width="48%"} ![](large.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"} ::: :::: ### Raster vs. vector formats When saving a figure, you have two fundamentally different types of format to choose from: - **Raster formats** (PNG, JPEG, TIFF) store the image as a grid of pixels.\ Quality is fixed at save time using the figure's "_resolution_" — but zoom in far enough and you'll see pixelation. Among these formats, TIFF is generally preferred for scientific publications while PNG may also work, while JPEG should typically be avoided. - **Vector formats** (SVG, PDF) store the image as geometric instructions — lines, curves, text.\ They render sharply at any size, and text is selectable and searchable. Among these formats, SVG is generally preferred or scientific publications. For publication, **vector formats are generally preferable** when the journal accepts them. Figures will be crisp at any print size, and editors or co-authors can make minor tweaks in a vector editor such as InkScape or Illustrator. To save to SVG format, we need the `svglite` package -- let's install that: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("svglite") # (We don't need to load it with library() because ggsave() will use it # automatically when we save to SVG) ``` ::: As mentioned above, the filetype is simply specified in `ggsave()` using the file extension. Earlier, we saved to PNG, so let's now practice saving to SVG: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave(filename = "large.svg", plot = p, width = 9, height = 6) ``` ::: ::: callout-note #### Controlling resolution for raster formats For raster formats (PNG, JPEG, TIFF), resolution of the figure is controlled with the `dpi` argument (dots per inch). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave( filename = "large_dpi300.png", plot = p, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300 ) ``` ::: A dpi of 300 is sufficient in almost any context, though some journals may request a resolution up to 600 dpi or so. :::
::: exercise #### {{< fa user-edit >}} Exercise: Compare formats Open the SVG file, and the two `large` PNG files, and compare how they look when you zoom in closely. :::: columns ::: {.column width="47%"} **PNG (at 300 dpi!)**: ![](img/png_zoom.png){width="90%" fig-align="center" .lightbox} ::: ::: {.column width="1%"} ::: ::: {.column width="52%"} **SVG**: ![](img/svg_zoom.png){width="90%" fig-align="center" .lightbox} ::: :::: :::
## Format individual words with _ggtext_ While you can set the fontface (plain, bold, italic, etc.) of axis titles, legend titles, and so on with `theme()`, you can't make individual words in these titles bold or italic with that approach. (The same is true for applying colors.) _ggtext_ is a package that lets you use Markdown and HTML markup to format text in ggplot2 plots. It is useful for things like bolding or italicizing individual words in a label, or coloring individual words to match data colors, which can make a legend unnecessary. Install it if you don't have it yet: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("ggtext") ``` ::: Then load it: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggtext) ``` ::: The main function in _ggtext_ is `element_markdown()`. It works as a drop-in replacement for `element_text()` in `theme()` calls, and enables Markdown and HTML parsing for that text element. Let's say for the sake of argument that we wanted to make parts of the axis titles bold and italic. The [Markdown syntax](https://www.markdownguide.org/basic-syntax/) to make text **bold** is `**text**` and `*text*` to make it *italic*. Without `element_markdown()`, the asterisks are printed literally: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p + labs(x = "**Flipper length** (*mm*)", y = "**Body mass** (*g*)") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Once we switch to `element_markdown()`, the formatting is applied: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p + labs(x = "**Flipper length** (*mm*)", y = "**Body mass** (*g*)") + theme( axis.title.x = element_markdown(), axis.title.y = element_markdown() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: `element_markdown()` can be used for any text element in `theme()`: axis titles, axis tick labels, plot titles, captions, and so on. As another example, say we wanted italic species names in the legend. First, note that if it's OK that everything becomes italic, we can just do that with `theme()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p + theme(legend.text = element_text(face = "italic")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: But what if we needed to make only one part of the text italic? We could do that once again with _ggtext_. First, let's define the labels we want to use for the legend, with Markdown syntax for italics: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species_labs <- c( "Adélie (*P. adeliae*)", "Chinstrap (*P. antarcticus*)", "Gentoo (*P. papua*)" ) ``` ::: Next, we can use these labels in the legend with `scale_fill_brewer()`, and then tell `theme()` to interpret the legend text as Markdown with `element_markdown()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2", labels = species_labs) + theme(legend.text = element_markdown()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Scale for fill is already present. Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale. ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ### Colored text! _(Click to expand)_ To color individual words, use an HTML `` tag with inline CSS: ```html some text ``` The most powerful application of this is coloring the species names in the plot title to match the data colors, making the legend redundant. First, let's find the three colors that `scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2")` assigns to the three species: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(3, "Dark2") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "#1B9E77" "#D95F02" "#7570B3" ``` ::: ::: The species factor levels in the penguins data are alphabetical (Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo), so those colors are assigned in that order. Now let's use them in the title: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p + labs(title = "**Adelie**, **Chinstrap**, and **Gentoo** penguins") + theme( plot.title = element_markdown(), legend.position = "none" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Because the title now carries the color information, we can choose to remove the legend with `legend.position = "none"`. :::
::: exercise #### {{< fa user-edit >}} Exercise: .... :::
## Adding images to plots If you want to follow along with the code below, you'll need to download the file `3penguins.png` from our GitHub repo: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} download.file("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jelmerpoelstra/ggplot2-course/master/img/3penguins.png") ``` ::: We'll also load two additional packages for working with images in R: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(grid) # Should always be installed library(png) # If you don't have it, install with install.packages("png") ``` ::: ### An image in a multi-panel layout First, we'll practice adding an image to a multi-panel layout, so basically treating the image as just another plot in the layout. Let's start with the same three-panel layout we had in the previous session: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_scatter <- penguins |> drop_na() |> ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(vars(species)) p_bar <- penguins |> ggplot(aes(x = island)) + geom_bar() p_box <- penguins |> drop_na() |> ggplot(aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g)) + geom_boxplot() p_scatter / (p_bar | p_box) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: First, we'll read the image in as a graphical object with `readPNG()` and `rasterGrob()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} img <- rasterGrob(readPNG("3penguins.png")) ``` ::: Next, we can add the image to the layout with _patchwork_'s `wrap_elements()` function: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Store the image as a plot element with wrap_elements(): p_img <- wrap_elements(panel = img) # Add the image to the layout with patchwork syntax: (p_scatter + p_img) / (p_bar | p_box) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-27-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Above, the plot with the image is quite small, and this is because the image is being resized to fit the plot _panel_ (that is, the area inside the axes of a plot). Note that it's taking up the entire plot panel height, but because the image has a 1:1 aspect ratio, it is not taking up the entire plot panel width. While that makes it appear even smaller, it's a good thing that the image's aspect ratio is respected, or the image would look stretched. Alternatively, we can tell `wrap_elements()` to use the entire _plot_ area for the image with `plot =` (above, we used `panel =`): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_img_big <- wrap_elements(plot = img) (p_scatter + p_img_big) / (p_bar | p_box) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} #### A different method with different image-fitting behavior A final alternative is to read the image in with the _magick_ package. That's an easy way to fix the aspect ratio of the image, such that patchwork will automatically take that aspect ratio into account when allocating space for each plot in the layout. First, install and then load the package: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("magick") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(magick) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Linking to ImageMagick 6.9.13.29 Enabled features: cairo, fontconfig, freetype, heic, lcms, pango, raw, rsvg, webp Disabled features: fftw, ghostscript, x11 ``` ::: ::: Now use _magick_'s `image_read()` to read the image in, and `image_ggplot()` to convert it to a ggplot object: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_img_fixed <- image_ggplot(image_read("3penguins.png")) (p_scatter + p_img_fixed) / (p_bar + p_box) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-31-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: ### An image inside a plot It seems to me that this is most easily done with the _cowplot_ package. This provides a `draw_image()` function that can be used in combination with `ggdraw()` to add images to ggplots. Install _cowplot_ if you don't have it already, then load it: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("cowplot") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(cowplot) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'cowplot' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:patchwork': align_plots ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:lubridate': stamp ``` ::: ::: Now, we can add the image to our plot with `ggdraw()` and `draw_image()`, where: - The `x` and `y` arguments specify the position of the image in the plot (between 0 and 1), with x being the left-most position and y being the bottom-most position. These are not the same as the x and y axes of the plot, but rather the position of the image relative to the plot area. - The `height` and `width` arguments specify the size of the image relative to the plot (between 0 and 1). One thing to note is that the aspect ratio of the image is preserved, so even if you specify a square image like we do below, the actual image may not be square if the original image is not square. While it may seem odd in that case to specify both width and height, when omitting one of these, the function somehow has strange behavior with respect to the `x` and `y` coordinates. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggdraw(p) + draw_image("3penguins.png", x = 0.1, y = 0.65, height = 0.35, width = 0.35) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-34-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ### An example with multiple images Say that we wanted to add a separate image for each penguin species in an appopriate part of the plot. First, download the following penguin images: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} #TBA ``` ::: And add them to the plot: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggdraw(p) + draw_image("adelie.png", x = 0.2, y = 0.6, height = 0.15, width = 0.15) + draw_image("chinstrap.png", x = 0.5, y = 0.25, height = 0.15, width = 0.15) + draw_image("gentoo.png", x = 0.4, y = 0.8, height = 0.15, width = 0.15) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ### Multiple images in a plot, with boxes _(Click to expand)_ I thought drawing colored boxes around the penguins images could be a useful example. This turned out to be more difficult than expected, so we won't cover it during the Code Club session. But for your and my future reference, I am including the code here. First off, I couldn't get this to work in combination with the `draw_image()` approach used above to insert the images. Instead, we'll have to switch to a _patchwork_-based approach. To insert a single image like we did above, that would look as follows: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Setting the image width as a variables width <- 0.35 # Read in the image raw <- readPNG("3penguins.png") img <- rasterGrob(raw, width = unit(1, "npc"), height = unit(1, "npc")) # Obtain the image's aspect ratio and compute the height from that: aspect_ratio <- dim(raw)[1] / dim(raw)[2] height <- width * aspect_ratio # Create the plot with the inserted image # (Setting the plot panel's aspect ratio to 1 seems necessary for the image's # aspect ratio to be correctly displayed.) p + theme(aspect.ratio = 1) + inset_element(img, right = 0.4, left = 0.4 - width, top = 1, bottom = 1 - height) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-38-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: That works, but you can hopefully see why I choose to use `draw_image()` instead for the earlier examples. Now, let's expand this to insert individual penguins' images with colored boxes: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Set the width and save the desired box colors width <- 0.15 colors <- RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(n = 8, name = "Dark2") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Adelie raw_ade <- readPNG("adelie.png") img_ade <- rasterGrob(raw_ade, width = unit(1, "npc"), height = unit(1, "npc")) aspect_ade <- dim(raw_ade)[1] / dim(raw_ade)[2] ht_ade <- width * aspect_ade box_ade <- ggplot() + theme_void() + theme(panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = colors[1], linewidth = 2)) # Chinstrap raw_chi <- readPNG("chinstrap.png") img_chi <- rasterGrob(raw_chi, width = unit(1, "npc"), height = unit(1, "npc")) aspect_chi <- dim(raw_chi)[1] / dim(raw_chi)[2] ht_chi <- width * aspect_chi box_chi <- ggplot() + theme_void() + theme(panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = colors[2], linewidth = 2)) # Gentoo raw_gen <- readPNG("gentoo.png") img_gen <- rasterGrob(raw_gen, width = unit(1, "npc"), height = unit(1, "npc")) aspect_gen <- dim(raw_gen)[1] / dim(raw_gen)[2] ht_gen <- width * aspect_gen box_gen <- ggplot() + theme_void() + theme(panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = colors[3], linewidth = 2)) p + theme(aspect.ratio = 1) + inset_element(img_ade, right = 0.2, left = 0.2 - width, top = 0.6, bottom = 0.6 - ht_ade) + inset_element(box_ade, right = 0.2, left = 0.2 - width, top = 0.6, bottom = 0.6 - ht_ade) + inset_element(img_chi, right = 0.7, left = 0.7 - width, top = 0.3, bottom = 0.3 - ht_chi) + inset_element(box_chi, right = 0.7, left = 0.7 - width, top = 0.3, bottom = 0.3 - ht_chi) + inset_element(img_gen, right = 0.6, left = 0.6 - width, top = 0.9, bottom = 0.9 - ht_gen) + inset_element(box_gen, right = 0.6, left = 0.6 - width, top = 0.9, bottom = 0.9 - ht_gen) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-40-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: :::
